task:
Our users are reporting that when using our CLI tool to open the local dashboard that using CTRL-C isn't actually stopping the dashboard from running. It seems to ignore the command. This following PR was proposed to fix it but there is at least one critical concern with the solution. Please pull it down, review, and clean it up so that it is professional, and enterprise-grade best practices are followed. Keep the scope to making it work professional and crisp. https://github.com/oddessentials/ado-git-repo-insights/pull/228. src/ado_git_repo_insights/cli.py
Comment on lines +1590 to +1591
            def _signal_shutdown(signum: int, frame: object) -> None:
                httpd.shutdown()
@chatgpt-codex-connector
chatgpt-codex-connector bot
1 hour ago
P1 Badge Avoid calling shutdown() from SIGINT handler thread

_signal_shutdown calls httpd.shutdown() from the same main thread that is executing httpd.serve_forever(), which can deadlock on Ctrl+C. In Python’s socketserver.BaseServer, shutdown() blocks waiting for serve_forever() to finish and explicitly requires being called from another thread; when invoked inside the signal handler, the serve loop cannot progress to completion, so the CLI can hang instead of stopping.

task:
The new SIGINT handling introduces a runtime failure when the dashboard server is started outside the main thread, because Python forbids installing signal
  handlers there. That regression is enough to make the patch incorrect.

  Review comment:

  - [P2] Guard SIGINT registration to the main thread — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1603-1603
    Calling signal.signal() is only legal from Python's main thread. With this change, _run_http_server() now raises ValueError: signal only works in main
    thread of the main interpreter whenever cmd_dashboard is invoked programmatically from a worker thread (for example from a test harness, IDE integration, or
    any embedding application). Before this patch the server could still run in that scenario, so the new unconditional handler registration turns a previously
    valid call path into an immediate failure. I’d handle it by making SIGINT registration conditional, not removing it outright.

  signal.signal() only works in the main thread, so _run_http_server() should install the temporary handler only when threading.current_thread() is
  threading.main_thread(). In non-main-thread callers, skip registration and let the server run as it used to. That preserves the Windows/IDE shutdown
  improvement for normal CLI use without breaking embedded or programmatic use.

  Concretely, the shape should be:

  - compute can_install_sigint = threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()
  - only call signal.getsignal(...), signal.signal(...), and restore the original handler inside that branch
  - keep serve_forever() outside the branch so behavior is unchanged otherwise

  I would avoid “try signal.signal() and catch ValueError” as the primary approach unless you want extra defensiveness; the thread check is clearer and
  expresses intent better.

  I’d also add a regression test for “dashboard server invoked from a worker thread does not raise ValueError”, because this is exactly the
user_feedback_text:
The new SIGINT handling introduces a runtime failure when the dashboard server is started outside the main thread, because Python forbids installing signal
  handlers there. That regression is enough to make the patch incorrect.

  Review comment:

  - [P2] Guard SIGINT registration to the main thread — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1603-1603
    Calling signal.signal() is only legal from Python's main thread. With this change, _run_http_server() now raises ValueError: signal only works in main
    thread of the main interpreter whenever cmd_dashboard is invoked programmatically from a worker thread (for example from a test harness, IDE integration, or
    any embedding application). Before this patch the server could still run in that scenario, so the new unconditional handler registration turns a previously
    valid call path into an immediate failure. I’d handle it by making SIGINT registration conditional, not removing it outright.

  signal.si

task: [Request interrupted by user for tool use]
[Request interrupted by user for tool use]

The new SIGINT handling introduces observable regressions: it can swallow Ctrl+C during browser launch, and it suppresses any pre-existing SIGINT handler for
  the lifetime of the server. Those are behavior changes that can break real startup and embedding scenarios.

  Full review comments:

  - [P2] Delay SIGINT override until after browser launch — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1607-1610
    When open_browser=True, the custom SIGINT handler is installed before webbrowser.open(url) runs. In that startup window, pressing Ctrl+C no longer raises
    KeyboardInterrupt; it only queues httpd.shutdown(), which cannot interrupt a blocking or hung webbrowser.open() call. This regresses the CLI's ability to
    abort startup on systems where browser launching is slow or misconfigured.
  - [P2] Preserve existing SIGINT handlers while the server is running — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1599-1607
    This replaces the process-wide SIGINT handler with _request_shutdown and never invokes the previously registered handler. In any embedding/test harness that
    installs its own SIGINT cleanup, pressing Ctrl+C during the dashboard run will now skip that cleanup entirely and only stop the HTTP server. Because
    signal.signal() is global, this changes behavior outside the dashboard command itself.
user_feedback_text:

    KeyboardInterrupt; it only queues httpd.shutdown(), which cannot interrupt a blocking or hung webbrowser.open() call. This regresses the CLI's ability to
    abort startup on systems where browser launching is slow or misconfigured.
  - [P2] Preserve existing SIGINT handlers while the server is running — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1599-1607
    This replaces the pr

task:
The SIGINT wrapper changes behavior for processes that had already configured SIGINT to be ignored or handled by the OS default action. That regression makes
  the new shutdown logic unsafe for embedded use cases, so the patch is not fully correct as written.

  Review comment:

  - [P2] Preserve existing SIGINT ignore/default semantics — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1609-1618
    If _run_http_server() is embedded in a process that has already set SIGINT to SIG_IGN or SIG_DFL, pressing Ctrl+C will now still stop the dashboard because
    _request_shutdown() always calls httpd.shutdown() before checking the previous handler. That changes the caller's explicitly configured signal policy and
    makes the new handler unsafe to use from hosting applications that intentionally ignore or delegate SIGINT. Do not push.
user_feedback_text:
The SIGINT wrapper changes behavior for processes that had already configured SIGINT to be ignored or handled by the OS default action. That regression makes
  the new shutdown logic unsafe for embedded use cases, so the patch is not fully correct as written.

  Review comment:

  - [P2] Preserve existing SIGINT ignore/default semantics — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\src\ado_git_repo_insights\cli.py:1609-1618
    If _run_http_server() is embedded in a process that has already set SIGINT to SIG_IGN or SIG_DFL, pressing Ctrl+C will now still stop the dashboard because
    _request_shutdown() always calls httpd.shutdown() before checking the previous handler. That changes the caller's explicitly configured signal policy and
    makes the new handler unsafe to use from hosting applications that intentionally ignore or delegate SIGINT. Do not push.
